then.verdict: Your next checkpoint: get an I-140 approved — by any road
then.headline: On H-1B with the green card not locked in, the approved I-140 is the single document that changes your life math
why: An approved I-140 unlocks H-1B extensions beyond year 6 in 3-year increments — the visa clock stops being an existential threat.
It locks your priority date: even if you change employers later, the date can travel with you (priority-date retention).
Ask your employer THIS quarter for the PERM/I-140 timeline in writing. 'We'll start next year' costs you a year of Indian-born EB2/EB3 queue position that compounds for a decade.
If PERM is paused or your employer won't file: evaluate an EB2-NIW self-petition — you control it, no employer needed, and premium processing is available for NIW I-140s (verify current USCIS list).
In parallel, start EB1A evidence-building now. EB1A is current or years faster for India-born applicants; even a 'someday' EB1A file changes today's decisions.
pros: Every path here (PERM, NIW, EB1A) ends at the same document; you can run more than one at once
Priority date is an asset you own once I-140 approves
cons: NIW/EB1A standards are real bars, not paperwork formalities

worst_case.scenario: Year 5 on H-1B, employer never filed, layoff hits, and the 60-day clock starts with no I-140 to port
worst_case.mitigation: The whole point of acting this quarter: an approved I-140 (even from a past employer) preserves extensions and your date. Attorney-verify your personal case.

then.verdict: EB1A: pick 3 of 10, then argue them well
then.headline: The extraordinary-ability green card is a portfolio you build, not a lottery you enter
why: You need to satisfy at least 3 of USCIS's 10 criteria, then win the 'final merits' judgment that the whole picture shows sustained acclaim.
Most strong tech/product cases run on the same 4–5 criteria: judging, original contributions, leading/critical role, scholarly/professional articles, high remuneration.
Evidence compounds: one conference talk becomes 'judging' (panel), 'published material' (coverage), and 'leading role' (invited expert) if you log it properly.
For India-born applicants, EB1 has run years ahead of EB2/EB3 — this is the difference between a green card this decade and next.
pros: Self-petitioned: no employer permission, survives job changes
The evidence-building also advances your career either way
cons: The final-merits bar is genuinely high — 3 weak criteria lose
12–24 months of deliberate evidence-building for most profiles

worst_case.scenario: A rushed file on thin evidence draws an RFE, then a denial, and you've spent filing fees + attorney fees + a year
worst_case.mitigation: Track criteria honestly for 6–12 months BEFORE filing; file when 3 are strong and 2 more are building. Denial doesn't bar refiling stronger.

then.verdict: Day-1 CPT: legal on paper, expensive at every next door
then.headline: Day-1 CPT enrollment keeps you 'in status' while quietly raising the risk on every future filing
why: The risk isn't day one — it's later: H-1B extensions, transfers, and especially consular stamping are where Day-1 CPT histories draw RFEs and 221(g)s.
Officers distinguish 'maintaining status' from 'maintaining credibility': a second master's whose main feature is work authorization invites exactly that scrutiny.
If the school is ever flagged (see past university shutdowns), everyone who attended carries that history permanently.

Real alternatives exist: cap-exempt H-1B employers (universities, affiliated nonprofits, research orgs), O-1 exploration if your evidence is building, or a planned year home with a clean re-entry strategy.
pros: Genuinely accredited programs with real coursework exist in the Day-1 CPT world — the category isn't uniformly fraudulent
cons: Elevated RFE/denial exposure at extension, transfer, and stamping
Visa officers at consulates apply the harshest lens
The history never leaves your file

worst_case.scenario: H-1B approved, you travel for stamping, and the consulate issues a 221(g) digging into your CPT employment — you're stuck outside the US for months
worst_case.mitigation: If you already have Day-1 CPT history: document genuine coursework and employer-study nexus meticulously, and do NOT travel for stamping without an attorney reviewing the file first
